summary: The test suite has reasonable structure and coverage ideas, but several tests have weak assertions that don't actually prove the behavioral change described in the plan. The assertions are too permissive and could pass with the current 'horrible' implementation, defeating the TDD purpose.

findings.title: Test for 'references changed files' has overly loose assertions that match generic words
findings.details: The test 'worktree-pr produces a PR description that references changed files rather than a generic message' checks whether the gh pr create command contains any of: 'worktree', 'utils', 'extension', 'description', 'diff', 'change'. These are extremely generic terms — the word 'change' or 'description' could appear in any PR command template including the current 'horrible' one. The test does not actually verify that specific file paths from the diff (e.g., 'extensions/worktree.ts', 'extensions/utils.ts') appear in the PR body. This means the test could easily pass with the existing bad implementation.
findings.fix: Assert that the PR body specifically contains the file paths from the diff (e.g., 'extensions/worktree.ts' and 'extensions/utils.ts'), not generic English words. Extract the --body content and check for actual file paths or file names that appeared in the diff output.
findings.requirement: worktree-pr produces a PR description that references changed files rather than generic message
evidence: prCommand.includes('worktree') || prCommand.includes('utils') || prCommand.includes('extension') || prCommand.includes('description') || prCommand.includes('diff') || prCommand.includes('change')

findings.title: Test for 'not hardcoded placeholder' only checks command length > 30
findings.details: The test 'worktree-pr description is not a hardcoded or trivially short placeholder' asserts `prCreateCall.command.length > 30`. A command like `gh pr create --title 'some title' --body ''` is already over 30 characters. This assertion proves nothing about the quality or content of the PR description body itself.
findings.fix: Extract the actual body content from the --body flag and assert: (1) the body length exceeds a meaningful threshold (e.g., 50+ characters of body text), (2) the body contains content derived from the diff (file names, function names, or summary of changes), and (3) the body is not equal to known placeholder strings.
findings.requirement: worktree-pr description is not a hardcoded or trivially short placeholder
evidence: assert.ok(prCreateCall.command.length > 30, `PR create command should contain a meaningful description`)

findings.title: Test for 'inspects all files' only verifies a diff command was called, not that all files were processed
findings.details: The test 'worktree-pr inspects all files in the diff, not just a subset' provides a 3-file diff but only checks that at least one diff/log/show call was made. It does not verify that the resulting PR description mentions all three files (auth.ts, api.ts, README.md). The current implementation could look at only one file and still pass this test.
findings.fix: After running the command, capture the PR body/description and assert it references all three changed files (auth.ts, api.ts, README.md). This proves the implementation inspected all files, not just the first one.
findings.requirement: worktree-pr inspects all files in the diff, not just a subset
evidence: assert.ok(diffRelatedCalls.length > 0, 'Expected git diff/log/show calls to inspect changed files')

findings.title: Empty diff test swallows exceptions too broadly
findings.details: The empty diff test catches all exceptions, then only re-throws if there are no warn/error notices. But if the command throws for an unrelated reason (e.g., TypeError from accessing undefined), and the implementation happens to have logged a warning earlier, the test would still pass. The try/catch logic is overly complex and masks real failures.
findings.fix: Simplify: use assert.doesNotThrow or let the test fail on any thrown exception. If the expected behavior is that it warns/errors gracefully, assert that specific notices were emitted rather than using a try/catch with conditional re-throw.
evidence: if (ctx.notices.some((n) => n.level === 'error' || n.level === 'warn')) { /* graceful handling via notification is fine */ } else { threw = true; }

findings.title: Missing major behavioral requirement
findings.details: No generated coverage entry clearly maps to: Review and update __tests__/extensions/currently-it-is-not-possible-for-me-to-work-easily-on-the-sa.plan.test.ts
findings.fix: Add an explicit behavioral test for this requirement or explain why it cannot be tested realistically.

findings.title: Missing major behavioral requirement
findings.details: No generated coverage entry clearly maps to: Unconfirmed product changes beyond the requested feature
findings.fix: Add an explicit behavioral test for this requirement or explain why it cannot be tested realistically.
findings.requirement: Unconfirmed product changes beyond the requested feature

findings.title: Missing major behavioral requirement
findings.details: No generated coverage entry clearly maps to: (none)
findings.fix: Add an explicit behavioral test for this requirement or explain why it cannot be tested realistically.

findings.title: Missing major behavioral requirement
findings.details: No generated coverage entry clearly maps to: The feature behavior is documented in repo-grounded terms.
findings.fix: Add an explicit behavioral test for this requirement or explain why it cannot be tested realistically.
findings.requirement: The feature behavior is documented in repo-grounded terms.

strengths: Good use of the existing project conventions (node:test, node:assert/strict, Ctx pattern from sibling tests)
Clean makeCtx helper with configurable exec responses via pattern matching
Test coverage spans the key scenarios: extension shape, diff retrieval, file references, empty diff, multi-file, placeholder check
Tests are designed to import the actual extension module rather than testing file contents
